chapters.title: Chair Linda Lee opens DOF hearing and welcomes Commissioner Lee

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: church back in the day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So fun facts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you guys ready?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hello, Commissioner Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Annyeong, chingu.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so welcome to our third hearing of today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and welcome to our familiar face, Commissioner Richard Lee and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a reminder, my usual PSA before each section of the hearings, we're doing all day public day testimony tomorrow starting at 9 30 so make sure you sign up have folks sign up public testimonies all day tomorrow starting at 9 30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I think that's actually it on my part.

utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to pass it off to Brian Salfo, our amazing committee counsel, to administer the youth.

utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee?

utterances.text: I do.

utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Scher?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner James?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff

chapters.summary: Commissioner Richard Lee introduces himself and his team, outlines DOF's central role collecting over $50 billion in revenue, and describes his guiding values of fairness, customer service, clarity, and accuracy.
chapters.title: Commissioner Lee introduces DOF mission and FY27 executive budget overview

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Lee, members of the Finance Committee and members of the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: My name is Richard Lee.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I am the Commissioner of the New York City Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today on DOF's fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I am joined today by First Deputy Commissioner Jeffrey Shear to my left and Jacqueline James, our Chief Financial Officer and Deputy Commissioner for Administration and Planning to my right.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm grateful for their leadership and their deep commitment to this agency.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This is my first opportunity to appear before you as Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I am honored to lead an agency that plays a central role in the life of the city.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Finance collects the revenue that makes the city government possible.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We assess more than 1 million properties, administer tax benefits and relief programs, collect taxes and other charges, adjudicate parking summonses,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: record property documents, and provide services that touch homeowners, renters, small businesses, and many others.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: DOF collects more than $50 billion in taxes and other revenues in fiscal year 2025 alone.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Since joining DOF, I have missed

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: by the expertise and dedication of the people who work here.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I've also been clear with our agency staff about the people who work, about the values that I want to guide our work, fairness, customer service, clarity, and accuracy.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Most New Yorkers interact with DOF because they have an obligation, a question, or problem that they might find unpleasant.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It is our duty to ensure that those interactions are as helpful and efficient as possible.

chapters.summary: Lee reports DOF completed a comprehensive review of ECB debt collections, recovering over $9 million from Amazon for unpaid vehicle idling violations, and outlines plans for an ECB amnesty program in FY27 pending council authorization.
chapters.title: Commissioner Lee on ECB debt review and Amazon collection success

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Since our preliminary budget testimony in March, DOF has advanced several important priorities for this administration.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: First, we completed a comprehensive review of the city's collection of ECB debt.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So ECB debt includes unresolved violations issued by several agencies that may have become judgments and have been referred to DOF for collection.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Following that review, the city successfully recovered more than $9 million from Amazon for unpaid vehicle idling violations, an important example of what it means to enforce the city's laws fairly and effectively.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Just as important, this review has given us a clear understanding of how we can strengthen collections going forward.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This includes improving coordination across agencies, using data more efficiently, and identifying cases where enforcement can produce meaningful results.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We'll continue to build on this work, including an ECB amnesty program in FY27 upon authorization by the council.

chapters.summary: Lee details $22 million in FY26 and $29 million in FY27 savings, the final property tax roll of 1.2 million properties, the new Mayor's Office of Deed Theft Prevention, and new DOF leadership appointments including Sheriff Edwin Raymond.
chapters.title: Commissioner Lee on DOF savings, property tax roll, and new administration initiatives

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Second, DOF has identified and implemented both operational and tax expenditure savings totaling more than $22 million in the current

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: fiscal year, and $29 million in fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: At a time of significant fiscal pressure, DOF remains focused on protecting the city's revenue base and ensuring that public dollars are used as intended.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Third, DOF issued a final property tax roll reflecting a valuation of more than 1.2 million properties and supporting the city's single largest source of revenue.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This work is highly technical, but underpins the property tax system and the services New Yorkers rely on every day.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We have also supported several major administration priorities since March.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani established the Mayor's Office of D-Def Protection, led by Peter White, the city's first office dedicated to coordinating prevention, outreach, enforcement, and victim support related to D-Def.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The new office will coordinate work of the City Records Office, the Sheriff's Office, and various other DOF divisions, while also providing new services and resources to vulnerable homeowners.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We look forward to continuing our partnership with City Hall, law enforcement, the courts, elected officials, and community partners.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The administration has also brought on new DOF leadership.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Omar Qadir has been appointed to lead the Mayor's Office of Pension Investments, MOPI.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani has also appointed Edwin Raymond as the new Sheriff of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The Sheriff's Office carries out critical civil and judicial enforcement responsibilities, and we're grateful for Sheriff Raymond as he assumes leadership of the office.

chapters.summary: Lee outlines the $373.85 million FY27 executive budget, onboarding 50 auditors increasing audit revenue by $50 million, the paused 2026 tax lien sale, and plans to implement the pied-a-terre surcharge.
chapters.title: Commissioner Lee on FY27 budget, auditor hiring, lien sale pause, and pied-a-terre surcharge

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Looking ahead, we have several priorities for the coming year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The first, as always, is the efficiency of our own operations.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: DOS fiscal year 27 executive budget is $373.85 million.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This is a slight change from the preliminary budget and reflects the savings that I referenced earlier.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are in the process of onboarding 50 auditors to strengthen tax enforcement and ensure that the city's laws are administered with fairness and accountability.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: An effective tax audit and enforcement division is central to our mission, and this investment underscores the administration's commitment to efficient revenue collection.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: As a result of this initiative, as well as better retention of existing auditors, the projection for the audit revenue has been increased by $50 million for fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We'll continue to work with the council on the creation of the land bank and a new shared vision for property tax enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: As the council knows, the mayor paused the tax lien sale for 2026 to allow for a comprehensive review of next steps.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: That pause gives us an opportunity to think carefully about the tools the city should have to resolve delinquency, protect homeowners, and support neighborhood stability.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We'll also take the lead in implementing the proposed pay-to-tear surcharge, which was included in the recently passed New York State budget to close the city's budget gap while protecting core services.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And finally, we'll continue to improve how we communicate with and serve New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Whether it's a

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Someone's applying for a property tax exemption, making a payment, contesting a parking ticket, recording a deed, or trying to resolve debt.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: They deserve a process that is clear, accurate, accessible, and respectful.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: In closing, I want to thank the council for your partnership and oversight.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I know you share a commitment to administering the city's tax laws and revenues laws fairly, collecting what is owed, connecting New Yorkers to help when they need it, and improving the experience of every New Yorker who comes to their city government for assistance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and I'm happy to answer any questions that you might have.

chapters.summary: Linda Lee notes the arrival of Deputy Speaker Williams, Majority Whip Hanks, and Councilmembers Brewer, Maloney, Wong, and Narcisse. She then opens questioning on property tax reform, noting promises by successive mayors have gone unfulfilled.
chapters.title: Chair Lee notes council members present and begins property tax reform questions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I forgot to mention, we've been joined by Deputy Speaker Williams, Majority Whip Hanks, Councilmember Brewer, Councilmember Maloney, and Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And sorry, I was getting a little teary-eyed hearing you testify.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry, because I'm very proud of you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to say that again, so I'm getting a little emotional.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But yes, very proud of you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, property tax reform.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With that, we'll get into questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh boy, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So property tax reform, and this is something we've all talked about before, has been promised by mayor after mayor, but nothing has happened.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And fundamentally, everyone believes that it needs to be reformed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this obviously lies with your agency, and you have a lot of the key data and expertise that will be required to develop a workable

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: solution.

chapters.summary: Lee asks when a property tax reform plan will be released. Commissioner Lee says the administration is building off the advisory commission's recommendations and hopes to advance reform in the next state legislative session, confirming state legislative changes are needed.
chapters.title: Chair Lee and Commissioner Lee on property tax reform timeline and state action

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Months ago this administration said it would form a plan that would be released within weeks and so just wanted to know when we can expect a property tax reform plan and what can you tell us what it may or may not look like.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So, May Ramdani has been long clear about the importance of property tax reform and fixing the inequities in our system.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: That's why we're busy at work right now to craft that proposal.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It's going to largely build off of the recommendations from the advisory commission on property tax reform.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Which clearly lays out a lot of the structural reforms that are needed to make the system more equitable, less regressive, and more transparent.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And this is looking at it from a holistic view, how the impacts of one class and changes to one class or one type of property are going to impact the building types and the property types in other classes.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: How if we move the class two co-ops and condos to a different system, how that will impact the small rentals, large rentals, commercial properties.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So we're looking at this very holistically and we'll hopefully be able to do this in the next state session.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then the next state session, okay, which is consistent with what OMB had mentioned as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as we know, it definitely impacts the outer boroughs and certain parts, like I know Deputy Speaker Williams talks about this a lot in Southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So do you believe that real tax reform can be done on the local level, or I'm assuming, I know the answer, or does it need to be done?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: State action.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because you have to wait until the next state session.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, we believe that the reform, the necessary steps to get to final and the best status of reform will be state legislative changes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're holding you to that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $4.9 million in PS savings and a baseline reduction of 12 positions. Commissioner Lee and CFO Jackie James explain the savings reflect timing adjustments from delayed hiring, and the position cuts are non-revenue-generating managerial roles absorbed internally.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DOF on FY26 savings and position reductions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so for savings, there are a number of savings that were taken as part of the directed savings plan from Executive Order 12.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this includes abatement compliance, OTPs savings, PS savings, STAR credit changes, and a vacancy reduction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: These all provide savings of $22 million in FY26 and $29.2 million in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There is a $4.9 million in PS savings for FY26.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you please explain where the savings are coming from?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So that reflects Department of Finance's 1.5 savings target related to the Chief Savings Officer's targets for FY26.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: A lot of it reflects the timing adjustments for when we were able to bring on new hires and implement some of the programs that were previously added.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm happy to have our Chief Financial Officer, Jackie James, maybe add a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: It had to do with some programs that we got funded for early and beginning of the year, and it did not get implemented until like six months later.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: So those were savings we were able to offer up to OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and there is a baseline reduction of 12 positions from DOF for savings of $1.2 million starting in FY26.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What are these positions, and will DOF see any change?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: changes in services provided to the public?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So we work closely with OMB to identify the vacancies that were reduced without impacting any operations on customer service or revenue collections.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: These are all non-revenue generating managerial positions and we're working internally to absorb the workload.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $13 million in additional revenue from co-op condo mailings. Commissioner Lee explains DOF identified recipients who may not qualify as primary residents and sent notices requesting proof of residency to ensure the abatement targets intended owners.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on co-op condo abatement compliance and $13 million revenue

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we're also going to see $13 million per year in additional revenue from co-op condo mailings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you please explain the source of the revenue and how it will impact co-op condo residents?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so the co-op and condo abatement is limited to primarily resident unit holders.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And using the latest available income tax information, DOF tax policy, identified a certain subset of population of recipients that may not actually qualify as primary residents.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so DOF sent out notices requesting proof of residency.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The estimate that you mentioned, the $13 million, is the projected result of that notification, including anticipated owners who would work with us to reestablish approved residency status as a result of those mailings.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Through this initiative, we are ensuring that the program is targeting the owners as intended to assist.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee questions the breakdown of $573 million collectible and $744 million uncollectible ECB debt. Commissioner Lee explains uncollectible debt involves respondents who cannot be identified, bankrupt businesses, or those lacking financial ability to pay.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on ECB outstanding debt: $1.3 billion total and collectibility categories

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving on to ECB violations, we discussed ECB violations and outstanding debt at length, and we were happy to see one of your first actions as commissioner was to announce a 30-day review to increase...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Collections of Outstanding Debt.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this has been an important issue for the council, as you know, in recent years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in your announcement, DOF identified that $573 million in outstanding debt is potentially collectible and an additional $744 million is deemed uncollectible for a total outstanding debt of $1.3 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yes, that is correct.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This is as of beginning of fiscal year 2026.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Again, as you mentioned, we did identify $573 million in outstanding debt that is potentially collectible, an additional $744 million that is deemed uncollectible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how did you break down the categories of what's collectible or not collectible?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So when, in some cases, when debt is categorized as uncollectible, it's because of the respondents that cannot be sufficiently identified or verified

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: and limits DOF's ability to pursue outreach and any other enforcement action.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: In other cases, it might be because the business that received it might have gone bankrupt and no longer exists, or they might just simply lack the financial ability to satisfy the outstanding debt.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So that's largely the main reasons why we have an uncollectible category.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $70.1 million collection target and whether DOF will set higher targets. Commissioner Lee explains the FY27 target plus $25 million from the amnesty program, and describes the 30-day review as an internal brainstorming to improve collections and compliance.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on ECB collection targets and the 30-day review rationale

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then your announcement of $70.1 million in collections is just 12% of the collectible debt.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So would you be willing to set targets for additional collections of outstanding debt in the fiscal year, and what would those targets be?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: right now sets our ECB collection target at about 70.1 million, as you stated, with an additional $25 million in collections driven by the ECB amnesty program, which was added at prelim.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And as we implement a lot of the findings that we have from our review, we're hoping that the process will better target some of these violations.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And should it be readjusted in the middle of the year, we're happy to do so.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and how much would you like to see collected in FY27?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Again, we're reviewing some of the processes internally to improve some of our collections.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And one of those examples is how we collected the Amazon $9 million.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We had a direct approach, sort of like a case management approach, of working directly with Amazon Logistics, walking them through the process, identifying issues that they might have to bring them into compliance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So initiating a lot of the strategies like that, plus others across the board internally, we're hoping to better the ECB collection.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're happy to update as we go along throughout the fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what was the rationale behind the 30-day review?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Oh, so our teams internally, we collect outstanding debt all the time, like every day.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Right, that's what I was wondering.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: That's the mission.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Essentially the mission of our agency led by Deputy Sheriff Annette Hill, who oversees our collections divisions.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The reason why we wanted to do a 30-day review is because we wanted to really encourage our entire agency, who's impacted by collections and administration, to think creatively.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: on what we can do to really enhance and improve collections and improve compliance across the board.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So it was an internal sort of brainstorming of how we can better it.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what findings emerged from the 30-day review. Commissioner Lee describes better targeting and classification, coordination with sister agencies like DOB, potential state legislation, and focusing outreach within the first year of violation issuance when collections are most responsive.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on ECB review findings and collection improvement strategies

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then what were some of those findings in terms of how to improve?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So some of the findings do include things like bettering targets and bettering classification, finding ways that we can tie and work with our sister agencies like DOB and other issuing agencies to tie to other programs or licensing that they might have.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And it includes in looking at state legislation that may be needed in order to improve some of our collections.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It is reaching out for our account executive unit to, again, do a more closer and touch with some of our high respondents

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: and figure out ways that we can better target the timing of some of these violations.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The largest collection of violations come in within the first year of the issuance of the violation.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So we're trying to better time it so that we can target in the first year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about actual rental payments received from the Port Authority for FY26, noting the preliminary budget included only $162.4 million. Commissioner Lee says he has the report and will pull it up, offering to work with OMB on any needed adjustments.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on airport rental revenue from Port Authority

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, just in case to cover myself, we've been joined by Councilmember Narcisse, because I wasn't sure if I mentioned you before.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, airport revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Preliminary budget included just $162.4 million in rental revenue from the Port Authority for the city's airports in FY26, and at the time, the Council believed that this was underestimating actual rental payments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So as Commissioner, you received the Port Authority's transmittal of rental payments in annual statements.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what was the amount of actual rental?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that the city received from the Port Authority for FY26?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I can get back to you on that one.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We have the report.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I just haven't, I could pull it up in a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But we're happy to work with OMB if any changes need to be made.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But we have received it.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'll get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Let me just pull it up right now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about a 10-year miscalculation of Section 1127 returns that resulted in over $100 million in lost revenue. Commissioner Lee explains the STAR benefit was incorrectly applied on both state and city sides after a 2016 shift, the error was discovered during an internal review, and corrections apply only to tax year 2025 with no clawback for prior years.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Section 1127 miscalculation affecting 40,000 city employees

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, so moving on to Section 1127 returns.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As a condition of city employment, any employee who is not a resident of New York City is required to make a payment equivalent to what their New York City income tax would have been if they were a city resident.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It is the council's understanding that DOF has been incorrectly calculating Section 1127 returns for 10 years, which has resulted in more than $100 million in lost revenue to the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This error was recently fixed, which has led to some city employees seeing a reduction in their tax refund or even charges to their account as a means for the city to take.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The missing revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I feel like that impacts some folks here too.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you please explain how the 1127 bills were supposed to be calculated and what was wrong to lead to a 10-year miscalculation?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so the 1127 waiver is essentially a contractual condition of employment for employees who live outside of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The city charter requires that New York City employees who live outside the five boroughs have to pay the equivalent of their New York City personal income tax liability.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So each filing year, employees would...

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: then withhold a portion of the income.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so if they withhold, to cover this tax liability, so if they withhold greater than the tax liability, they get a refund.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: If they withhold less, then they'll have to pay into it.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: As part of that process, the school tax relief, the STAR benefit, was calculated against the city's personal income tax up until 2016.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: After 2016, it was shifted to be a credit that's calculated against your state personal income tax.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So they're essentially receiving on the state side, but our systems kept continuing to provide this on the city side as well.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So essentially they're receiving the STAR credit from both the state and the city.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Earlier this year, we made the discovery that the city was still calculating this benefit off the 1127 return.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We notified City Hall and then we recalculated the refunds and balances owed this year for this tax year for 2025.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: No other changes are going to be made on the Section 1127 for tax years before 2025.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so they're not going to be penalized for not having, I mean, for taking the...

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, we're only correcting it for this year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're not doing a clawback for any previous years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, how was the mistake discovered?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How was the mistake discovered?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Oh, so it was discovered while we were doing an internal review of the 1127 way before.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the exact amount of the lost revenue as a result?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It averages just about like $10 to $12 million a year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It's different per year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And to $12 million per year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Per year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then does DOF or OMB have any plans?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, you already answered that part.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So who is responsible now for the administration of the 1127 waiver?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: That still remains Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: DOF is the primary administrator of the 1127 waiver.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and how many employees are currently under 1127?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Lever?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Right now, I think it's just about 40,000.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about ongoing business tax refund processing for FY26 and FY25. Commissioner Lee confirms DOF expects to continue issuing refunds, with about 304 potential refunds totaling approximately $81 million by year's end, depending on timing and audit resolutions.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on business tax refunds for GCT and UBT

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then the business tax refunds.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: DOF has been processing refunds for the general corporation tax and unincorporated business tax since the beginning of FY26, with some of these refunds occurring back to FY year 25.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does DOF expect to continue issuing refunds for both sets of business taxes through the end of the fiscal year and into the next fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If so, how much does DOF anticipate issuing in refunds?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For both taxes by the end of the fiscal year 26?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So yes, we do expect to continue issuing refunds for both fiscal years 26 and 25.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: For many businesses, it's not actually that unusual to have pending refund requests, especially if tax filers are asking for an amendment or an extension or things like that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Some of the taxpayers also may be under DOF audit.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And we might not be issuing the tax refund.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: the refund until that's resolved.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so the latest business collection tax system right now report shows that we have about 304 refunds that could potentially be issued by the end of this year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Again, potential because it's really depending on timing.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But these refunds total about $81 million total.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks why it took DOF years to reflect 2019 rent stabilization law changes in valuations. Commissioner Lee explains market factors and reporting lags, and notes DOF increased capitalization rates by 50 basis points for FY27, reducing rent-regulated building market values by 3.3% and taxes by 1.3%.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on rent-stabilized property assessments and capitalization rate changes

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving on to the block-by-block housing plan and assessments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The administration's block-by-block housing plan says DOF recognized that 2019 changes to rent stabilization laws changed the economics of rent-stabilized properties.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in 2016, it made changes to the capitalization rates used in their valuation, reducing the property taxes of 15,000 properties by 1.3%.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So why did it take so long for DOF to reflect the economics of these buildings changed?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the change, because the change to the law was made in 2019.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wondering.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know, why the gap?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so DOF does share the concern over properly valuing these mostly rent regulated units.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So yes, the laws did change in 2019.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But the market factors that influence this take a little bit of time to be reflected.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And evaluations need to reflect actual changes rather than projections, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So that's a little bit of that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The assessment process also has a built-in lag a little bit to give property owners sufficient time to provide their required documents.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so DOS valuations, as an example for FY27 that we used, these income and expense reports for this FY27, were actually for the calendar year 2024.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Which we actually received in 25, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So it does take a little bit of time.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So there's a lag in the reporting system, and there is a little bit of market factors we have to sort of encapsulate.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But with that said, for the fiscal year 27 assessment, we did actually make a few changes.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: One of those changes was to increase the capitalization rate by 50 basis points.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And this higher capitalization rate just reduces the market value of the building.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm happy to have our first deputy commissioner at any additional.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Notes if there's any

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: No additional notes unless you have further questions, Chair.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how much additional relief rent-regulated buildings will receive in future years from the capitalization rate change. Commissioner Lee says he cannot specify future dollar amounts due to many interacting factors but commits to continued monitoring and fair assessment of rent-regulated properties.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on future tax relief from capitalization rate changes for rental buildings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just two more sort of follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So changes to market values are phased in over five years for most rental buildings, which implies the 1.3 reduction in taxes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This year will grow over to the next several years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you provide us with how more relief these buildings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: get in future years because of these changes?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So just to kind of rehash the higher capitalization rate that we utilize for this year's role,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: the rent-regulated buildings had their market value reduced by 3.3% on average, and the taxes reduced by about 1.3%.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the rent-regulated buildings are a top priority for this administration, and we're going to continue to evaluate the evaluation methodologies that are used to ensure that all of these buildings are taxed fairly.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But there are a lot of other factors that go into that calculation.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so I can't really say with certainty what percentage or what dollar amount will be attributed to this change in the future.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But we're going to continue working on this.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're going to ensure that the tax assessments are accurate and reflect the values to the best of our ability.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And we'll properly manage and closely monitor the sector of the rental market as the year goes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about a 4% decline in Class A office parcels and whether it signals office-to-residential conversion trends. Commissioner Lee says only 3 of 30 known office-to-residential conversions were Class A buildings, with most conversions going to other Class 4 uses, and the impact on class shares will be minimal.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Class A office parcel decline and conversion trends

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then one last area before I pass it off to colleagues is decline in Class A office parcels on FY27 final roll.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the final assessment roll shows a roughly 4% decline in the number of Class A office parcels, creating a loss of a little more than 7 million square feet of Class A office space,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: which results in a 1.5% decrease in taxable assessed value for this property class.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And these declines are outpacing...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The declines outpaced declines seen in the Class B office category, a class of office space that has been particularly well suited for office to residential conversions over the last couple years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So as we continue to see a flight to quality in the office market in Manhattan, does the reduction in Class A office space reflect a new trend in the office to residential conversion pool?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I think it might be a little bit difficult to predict.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Like we can't really presume some of these trends based on sort of like one year's worth of data.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And across all these sort of building categories, fewer than half of the conversions were actually to residential use.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Most of these conversions were into other tax, you know, class four uses like retail, hotel, warehouses, whatever it might be.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Of the 30 office buildings that we do know that were converted to residential, only three were in the Class A office buildings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Three?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, only three were actually in the Class A office buildings of the 30 office buildings that we do know were converted to residential.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and so how much more square footage do you expect Class A office space to lose in future assessment roles, and how does this adjust the makeup of what each property tax class may pay of the levy each year?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So it is a little bit difficult to speculate based on just one year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We'll continue to monitor this market.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The overall market.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: value of tax class four property has been growing over the last couple years, so the loss of office buildings that occurred, we don't really think there'll be a huge impact.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The impact will probably be minimal to the class shares.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But either way, we'll continue to monitor the conversions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams presses on whether the administration has formally submitted property tax reform proposals to the state. Commissioner Lee confirms no formal submission has been made yet but says reform remains a priority and the administration is building proposals off the advisory commission's recommendations.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams on property tax reform submissions to Albany

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm going to pass it off to our deputy speaker, followed by the majority whip.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I just wanted to follow up really quickly on Chair Lee's question about property taxes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I know a lot of it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Requires state action, but the mayor and the OMB director, after the prelim budget briefing, committed to submitting something to Albany.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like, when we've asked Albany, they say that it's the city that has to make recommendations, and, like, literally, out of Speaker Hastie's mouth.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So has the administration made any formal submissions to the state?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Regarding property taxes and if so like status anything you can add to that yes so i would say that property tax reform does remain a priority for the administration and the mayor

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: highlighting again some of the inequities in our property tax system i believe it was a priority at the onset of the year but as we got through the budget process when we had to close a historic 12 billion dollar

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Our budget gap.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: A lot of discussions that happened in Albany shifted towards that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are constantly in the process of reviewing property tax reform and some of the proposals that we'll have to go through and building off of the tax commission's recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So, but to formally say we have not submitted.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You haven't submitted.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would love for you all to like reprioritize this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because as you know, they're going to go home.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you have to try again for next session.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how DOF measures progress toward its goal of making 100% of services accessible to underserved communities by 2026. Commissioner Lee and CFO James describe outreach efforts and note the report is not yet finalized, with tracking initiatives in development alongside the new deed theft prevention office.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on DOF racial equity plan and accessibility benchmarks

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, moving to questions about racial equity.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So your agency's racial equity plan commits to ensuring that 100% of Department of Finance services become more

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: in quotes, it's easily accessible and readily available to New Yorkers, particularly in historically underserved communities by the end of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How is your agency measuring whether that goal is actually being achieved and what benchmarks are currently being used to track progress and are there any budget implications?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the Department of Finance is moving towards efforts to do outreach.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're focusing a lot on our mayor's office of D-DEFT.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're appreciative of course recommendations and reviewing what steps that we as an agency have to take to better align with the goals of the initiative.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, your agency submitted this.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So, again, I'll have our CFO, James, if you want to maybe go a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just want to understand, like, how you all are tracking the progress set by the goal that you guys put forward.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: So we're still working with the committee because the report has not been finalized yet.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's supposed to be finalized like literally in a couple of weeks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then you guys have a goal to be 100% by 2026.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: So we do have a community outreach program.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: We do have initiatives already that's in place.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: for tracking.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: We will, once the report is completely final, we'll be able to work with the D-DEFT group that was just created.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: It's still been established.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: And some of the other outreach opportunities that we have to meet the goals that we've put in the plan.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how DOF ensures digital systems don't create barriers for seniors and those with limited internet access. Commissioner Lee highlights business centers providing language access and disability services, and increased in-person community affairs outreach events.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on digital systems barriers for seniors and limited-internet residents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, your plan also referenced expanding the use of technology to improve customer service.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How is your agency ensuring that increased reliance on digital systems do not unintentionally create additional barriers for seniors and residents with limited digital literacy or New Yorkers with limited internet access?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I would say that for all of our systems, we ensure that all New Yorkers are able to reach our services, especially at our business centers.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Our business centers are a core piece of connectivity with the community, and all of our business centers, we ensure that we provide things like language access services, services for the hearing impaired, vision impaired.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so we are actively ensuring that all of our services are going to be met by those in need.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Outreach events handled by our community affairs division has had more in-person events more than ever in all of our communities.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And we're well aware that most of our, you know, Scree, Tree, Shihi, Dihi are primarily offline.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so we're working to improve that across the board and have more opportunities for people who might not have access to technology to be able to apply and get these benefits.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what barriers DOF has identified and what changes have been implemented. Commissioner Lee emphasizes information equity, noting that documents like the Notice of Property Value are difficult to understand, and commits to improving clarity in mailings and website communications.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on barriers identified in underserved communities and information equity

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, your plan also referenced identifying barriers to access and underserved communities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What barriers has the agency identified so far and what policy or operational changes have been implemented in response?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So I could answer that a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: You know, one of the core

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: goals that I've come on board with Department of Finance, and this is something I tell a lot of my staff, is that Department of Finance in the past, when we've been giving out information, it's not always understandable, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Information equity is a really big thing.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: If a person receives their NLPV, their notice of property value, it's really difficult to understand.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And for even myself, when I bought my first home, I looked at the thing, I didn't really know what it said, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so one of the core priorities that I have right now is in all of our information, whether it's through mailings or our website,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: that we're properly informing and bettering the equity of information so that people are able to better understand what their situation is, what opportunities they have, or what other pathways they might have in order to absolve any of the situations or be in a better position,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: accessing abatement programs and whatever it might be.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So I would say over the next year, I'm hoping to improve upon that so that our information equity outwards to communities who don't typically have that access are going to be in a better position.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So,

chapters.summary: Lee asks how DOF incorporates the history of redlining into current assessment policies. Commissioner Lee and First Deputy Commissioner Shear explain the consistent sales-based valuation methodology, and Shear notes that assessment caps disproportionately impact non-gentrifying neighborhoods with higher effective tax rates, underscoring the need for reform.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on redlining history and equitable assessment practices

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The agency acknowledges the historic impacts of redlining and inequitable property assessment practices.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How is your agency incorporating that historical context into current assessment policies, outreach efforts, and homeowner protections?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So I'll pass to our first deputy in a second, but our assessments, we utilize a very specific formula, and we look at properties in a very specific area in order to determine our assessments.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So for class one properties, we'll look at sales and comparable areas to determine what their market value is.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: From there, we calculate the assessed value based upon the assessment ratio target, and then apply the caps.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So it is a very sort of formulaic way that we do all of our assessments, whether you live in Southeast Queens or Northeast Queens, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the formula is the same in that it's all sales-based.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: For our co-op and condo market, again, that is in our class two, which goes back to reform a little bit, in that we have to utilize comparable

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: rents and utilize their income and expense information to determine what their market rate and their sales values are.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But we have a fairly consistent valuation methodology across the board, whether you live in Queens or Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'll let our first deputy maybe expand a little bit on our methodology.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Well, I would like to just emphasize what the commissioner was saying, that property tax reform is really a key.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: component here because while we have the same methodology, for example, for assessing class one homes throughout the city, the caps that limit how quickly

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: a home can have its assessed value increase have a disproportionate impact on

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: neighborhoods that are have not been gentrifying so those neighborhoods have

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: assessment ratios that are relatively high and so they have higher effective tax rates compared to neighborhoods that have been gentrifying

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: They have the values have been going up, but because of the caps, their values have not been keeping pace.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So that's why we think the conversation about property tax reform is so important.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: And that's why it's so important for us to do a comprehensive review and to get the.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Property tax reform right before we propose something formally to the legislature.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about additional protections beyond the current notice of recorded document program, referencing her bill passed last year. Commissioner Lee says the Deed Theft Prevention Office is examining recording processes and needed legislative changes to pause suspicious recordings, and offers to review her legislation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on deed fraud notification and recorded document program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, last question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I passed a bill requiring notifications when deeds or mortgages are recorded against someone's property.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In your racial equity plan, you included a long-term goal of increasing participation in the notice of recorded document program to protect homeowners from deed fraud.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Given the prevalence of deed theft, obviously this administration takes that seriously.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: concerns in communities of color and among elderly homeowners, what additional protections or outreach initiatives is the Department of Finance considering beyond the current program?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I just wanted to understand how that notice differs from the bill that I passed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If it's the same thing, if it's something different, just wanted to understand.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm happy to look at the bill one more time just to see if there's any differences, but I would say one of the key components of the Deaf Prevention Office is to look at a lot of these linkages, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: How are properties being recorded?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: What happens during that process?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: What legislative changes need to be made in order to pause these recordings if we think that there is some sort of fraud going on?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And how do we improve upon the processes internally, working with investigations in the sheriff's office, plus other divisions within the Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I have to look at the bill that you are proposing one more time to see if there's something that we can include, but the recording of these is something that we are highly...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That was last year when we passed all our bills, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Package of bills that passed through department, not department, but goes to your department, passed through the finance division last year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Then I'm happy to take a look at the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Majority of hangs followed by Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kamillah Hanks asks about the timeline for the administration's review of tax lien sale enforcement. Commissioner Lee says the six-month review ends this summer and the administration is ready to work with the council on creating a land bank, with board nominees due July 28th.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks on lien sale cancellation and six-month review timeline

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: What's up?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Did you miss us?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I did.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Always.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: It's so true.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So did we talk about lien sale cancellation a little bit?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: I'm going to ask it anyway.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The administration canceled lien sale this year pending a six-month review and potential reform to how the city enforces property taxes and water bills.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Can you provide a timeline of when we can expect to hear?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: What the new tax enforcement would look like?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the mayor has made it clear that this administration's priority is to stabilize and protect housing and to minimize displacement.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are taking this commitment seriously as we consider how to enforce the collection of outstanding property taxes

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: while making sure that we're holding accountable for those individuals to make sure that they're paying and doing everything that we can to address and reach out to those homeowners that are at risk ahead of a lien sale.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Right now we are undergoing a comprehensive review.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It is a six-month review on the current lien sale outreach and sale process as a whole in order to evaluate how to, essentially like how is enforcement could look like in this.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: New era.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It's a six-month review that ends this summer, and we're looking forward to discussions with the council and other partners as we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I would say that with the reform package that passed earlier this year, we're ready to work with the council on the creation of the land bank, and we're getting prepared to put in our nominees for the working group for this new land bank board,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: which I believe is due on July 28th.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Let's hope that that's a five borough task force.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I hope so too.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Yeah, me too.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks whether the lien sale pause has impacted the current tax lien trust and if the city will make up the revenue shortfall. Commissioner Lee says existing liens in the trust are unaffected, and that behavior changes from enforcement take time to appear in collections data.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks on lien trust activities and revenue shortfall from pause

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So has this pause impacted activities of the current tax lien trusts?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: For the current tax lien, no.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The current tax lien, the current liens in the trust right now are subject to the previous contracts and the authority has not been impacted by the pause in the lien sale for this year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But with that said, we're reviewing the entire enforcement ecosystem right now, including previously sold liens as part of our review.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So that leads me to my next question.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So with the postponement, do you believe that we'll make up that revenue shortfall next year?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We as an administration really need time to ensure that we're enforcing these taxes, property charges and water bills in an equitable and sustainable way that's aligned with the mayor's vision.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Generally, changing behavior from taxpayers are due to

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: enforcement and it takes time to show up in our collections data, but we'll monitor the situation and keep working with the budget office and the city council throughout the process and keep everyone abreast of any changes in valuation that we might see.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks whether the new administration will continue Operation Padlock to Protect against illegal cannabis shops. Commissioner Lee says the city remains committed to enforcement against unlicensed cannabis sales, with the sheriff leading operations and new Sheriff Raymond reassessing the approach.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks on Operation Padlock and illegal cannabis enforcement

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So I'm going to move on to Operation Padlock to protect.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So Operation Padlock to Protect was an initiative during the previous administration to lock up illegal marijuana shops on a large scale.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Will the new administration continue this operation or will it be altering its approach to illegal marijuana stores?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Well, the city is committed right now to continuing the enforcement on unlicensed cannabis sales, and this is in order to support the legal industry's growth and to ensure that we're fighting against dangerous products that are sold in our communities.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The sheriff will continue to lead these enforcement operations relating to all judiciary and civil law.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are undergoing a review of the operations across the entire sheriff's office and our new sheriff, Sheriff Raymond, will be reassessing sort of steps as we go forward.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: It's good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Good to see you too.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Narcisse, followed by Wong.

chapters.summary: Council Member Mercedes Narcisse asks about staffing for the new Office of Deed Prevention. Commissioner Lee reports one of five positions filled (director Peter White), with a deed ombudsperson pending and two more positions to be posted, and describes outreach to HPD, DYTA, district attorneys, and the state attorney general.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on Office of Deed Prevention staffing and interagency work

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee, and thank you, Commissioner Lee.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I'm happy to see you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But one thing I'm going to tell you, honestly, I miss you because I cannot come and bother you all the time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You can still bother.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's no problem.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: To tell you my vision.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So Nathan got it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So in the preliminary plan, DOF received the new position to create the Office of Deaf Prevention.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Can you please provide an update on how many positions have you fulfilled so far in the new office?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What specific work will be done with other city agencies?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And is there any need to increase the size of this office, or is five positions enough?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So, so far, so it's for five positions within the Office of Deaf Prevention that sits at the Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We have only filled one position so far, which is the director, Peter White.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are in the midst of appointing the Deaf Ombudsperson, so that should be coming soon.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And we'll be posting shortly for two more positions, the deputy director, which is pending, as well as a community coordinator.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: In terms of the work of the Office of Deaf Deaf, the office will primarily serve as a liaison across of our agency,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: across all our divisions, and working with enforcement entities and advocacy and legal groups, as well as other agencies to tie together a lot of the incoming that we have related to the deaf prevention.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Some of the other agencies that we do currently or we have already reached out to as part of this office has been HPD, DIFTA, the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: of mass engagement as well as beginning the stages to forge relationships and alliances with the district attorneys offices across the five boroughs as well as the state attorney general.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Your last question was if it's enough, right?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It was enough.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So right now we are budgeted for five.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're in the process of onboarding additional three.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're going to keep working within our agency.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: A lot of the work that we're doing for this office is really uplifting it from internal, utilizing all the resources that we have at DOF in our land records, office of taxpayer

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: advocate, and across the board to work within Department of Finance to help uplift it.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: If we do feel that there is an additional need, we're happy to get to that conclusion and reach out to the budget office should there be a need to do so.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks what homeowners, especially seniors, can do if they believe their assessment is wrong and how fast the process takes. First Deputy Commissioner Shear explains the notice of property value and appeal process, and says he will follow up on turnaround times for clerical error corrections.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on property assessment appeals process for seniors

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And when a homeowner believes their assessment is wrong, what can it do, especially the seniors?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's kind of like, do they have the staff in there to help them out to handle their situation?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so there is a full process for individuals once they receive their notice of property value to contest any market value to assess value.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'll let our first deputy commissioner kind of walk us through that process.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So all property owners receive a notice of property value in January of each year.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: That is at the same time we issue the tent of the roll and property owners can use that information to either come to us for

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: what we call clerical errors, or can appeal to the tax commission if they disagree with their valuation.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: With regard to...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How fast is the process?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How fast?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's just like something that takes long, special.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We're talking about the seniors that complain.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Sometimes we try to help in my office, but sometimes I end up in your office.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I want to know how fast they can get a result.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: I'll have to get back to you with how quickly we turn around the clerical errors, but we will get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks what role DOF plays in flagging suspicious ownership transfers. Commissioner Lee says statutorily DOF cannot prevent deed recordings meeting legal requirements, and hopes for state legislation to provide more authority. Shear adds that property owners can sign up for a notification program alerting them when deeds are recorded against their property.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on flagging suspicious ownership transfers and deed fraud

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know, I heard my deputy speaker was talking about the D-DEF, right?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Homeowner has specifically the seniors.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What role does finance play in flagging suspicious ownership transfer?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And is it enough?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What do you do from your office?
