utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Like if you realize it's a suspicious transfer.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So, statutorily, right now we don't have an ability to, if a person is recording a deed, if they are meeting all the

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: legal requirements, then we actually can't prevent that from happening.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: This is one of the legislative fixes that we're hoping to see in the state level to give us a little bit more authority in those cases.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Chair, one more time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: We also urge property owners to sign up for our notification program.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So that way they come to us, they give us their mailing address or their email address.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: And if there's a deed recorded against their property, we will notify them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I want to say thank you to you, too, because your team has been coming to my office to help me out.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And thank you for the work you're doing.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But whatever it knew, I want to know about it because I want to help.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Knarsey area happened to be having the highest of foreclosure indeed they're following so I just want to make sure I help out on that one

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks if DOF is ready to administer the pied-a-terre surcharge and how it affects seniors on fixed incomes. Commissioner Lee confirms DOF expects to meet the $500 million revenue target, and Shear clarifies the surcharge applies to second homes of non-residents, with payment plans available for those facing difficulty.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on pied-a-terre surcharge and seniors with fixed incomes

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: my time is limited if there is a pay to tear surcharge moves ahead is finance ready to administer it and what does it expected to collect

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: from your time and how does finance um through i mean treat a senior in a fixed income whose home value has soared on paper but whose income has not uh moved at all

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: so the um we are currently in the implement

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: implementation stages of the P.A.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: De Terre surcharge, and we are confident that we will be able to meet the revenue target of $500 million as a part that was included as part of the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry, your second part of that question was?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What do you do?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How do you move?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Like you're talking about the last one I'm interested in.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The income whose home value soared, but now the income is fixed.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They don't have no budget increase.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So how do you work with that?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so just to outline the contours of the program, right now the program is specifically designed so that for class one properties, those that are valued between five and 15 million

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: will see a surcharge rate of about 0.8% for 15 to 25 million and 1.05 and 25 million plus on their class one market value will receive a 1.3% surcharge.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: For those who are in co-ops and condos, we have a similar sort of structure of one to three at 4%, three to five at 5.25, and five million at 6.5.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We don't necessarily directly look at personal income.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Our first deputy commissioner is going to oversee the implementation phases of the Peter Tear Tax.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'm happy to have him maybe elaborate on some of the implementation that we have to do and the case of this.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Yes, so two other points that I want to make is first just

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: To emphasize the nature of the pied-a-terre surcharge.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So this is on people who own two or more homes.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Second.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Second home.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: Right, second home.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So if it's someone and they are cash poor and their home has soared in value, we understand that.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: We do want to make sure people understand that it would need to be their second home where they are not a resident.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: The other point that I would like to make on this is that we do offer payment plans.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: So if we have someone in that situation and they have a surcharge and they are having difficulty paying it, we can offer a payment plan to them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But they're period here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But regular seniors, I want you all to pay attention to that because a lot of them having difficulty holding their home.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And if there's anything that flag, if it's in my district, I want to know because I want to help.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We're happy.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: If there are any instances that are coming up that you see where we have secondary homeowners in this situation, we're happy to work with them on any sort of programs that we might have and assist in any way.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So just feel free to reach out to our office.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You can still bother him.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You can still go bother him.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, follow right, Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong asks about delays in trash bin reimbursement for STAR recipients and whether outreach was sufficient. Commissioner Lee says the program has been extended to September 8th and DOF is working with the sanitation department, and will provide the number of Queens homeowners who received relief.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong on trash bin reimbursement program and outreach

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Commissioner, I have two questions, one regarding the trash bin reimbursement and notice, and another regarding the co-op and condo abatement compliance.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: First question is that small property owners had to comply with the official bin rule in June 2026, which was later extended to after Labor Day.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Now, some star recipients were eligible for reimbursement support.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But my office would get phone calls that many of them are still waiting for these reimbursements.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is how many Queens homeowners actually received that relief and was their outreach strong enough?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because it seemed to me that people don't know about, still people don't know about this reimbursement program.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can we extend this past Labor Day?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So I'll have to get back to you on the exact number.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'll have to provide that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We have extended the program to September 7th.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: September 8th after holidays.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So we have extended the program.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We're working closely with the sanitation department.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But September 8th is the new deadline.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so we have extended it as per your advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for letting us know.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks how much of the $13 million co-op condo abatement compliance revenue is anti-fraud enforcement versus aggressive compliance against ordinary owners. Commissioner Lee explains it was a qualification review identifying non-qualifying recipients, with an opportunity for owners to prove compliance and be reinstated.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong on co-op condo abatement compliance revenue

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My second question is the co-op and condo abatement.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The executive plan assumes

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: a baseline of $13 million annually from the cooperative condominium abatement compliance revenue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Now, how much of that is like true anti-fraud enforcement, and how much comes from more aggressive compliance actions against ordinary property owners?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So I would say it's sort of neither of the two.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: What we did was we went through

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: the qualifications and we went through the list and we noticed that several of the population wouldn't actually qualify for it.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: And so we sent out those notices to those homeowners.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: There is an opportunity for them to then prove their compliance and we're happy to then reinstate them into the program.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: But it is because we found a certain subset of the population that we felt that they actually didn't qualify and we want to make sure that we're providing this benefit to the people that actually qualify.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about enforcement and compliance plans for the pied-a-terre surcharge, especially for properties held through LLCs or trusts. Commissioner Lee says DOF is promulgating rules and the legislation provides a range of enforcement actions, with Shear noting the surcharge will appear on property tax bills to drive voluntary compliance.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong on pied-a-terre enforcement for LLCs and trusts

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I think I have time for squeeze one more question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The peer a tier collection.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The executive plan books 500 million from this peer-to-peer tax.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What is your enforcement and compliance plan, especially for properties held through LLCs or trusts?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So the peer-to-peer surcharge right now,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We are in the process of promulgating a lot of those rules.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The legislation does provide us a wide range of enforcement actions that we can utilize.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So as we get towards the rulemaking process, we're going to...

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Develop a lot of those enforcement actions a little bit more closely.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Again, our first deputy commissioner is overseeing the liens, the penitentiary surcharge and implementation process.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: If there's anything that I've missed.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: I would just add that the surcharge will be appearing on the property tax bill.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Shear
utterances.text: of the homeowners who are subject, we think that alone is going to drive voluntary compliance.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks about the SCRIE/DRIE increase from $50,000 to $75,000 and how it will be implemented. Commissioner Lee defers to City Hall and OMB on the budget action, noting local law is required, but says DOF already runs the program and expects to administer it quickly once adopted.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on SCRIE/DRIE increase and implementation

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Jacqueline James
utterances.text: Thanks, Comrade Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Good luck with that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: They all put, I know them all, they put their uncle, their brother, their sister in there pretty regularly.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I know most of the buildings.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about the land bank working group timeline. Commissioner Lee says nominees are due July 28th and the process will follow, though he cannot give a full timeline. Brewer then asks about ECB amnesty and collection agency effectiveness, with Lee describing the Amazon collection approach and plans to work down the list of highest violators.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on land bank timeline and ECB debt collection strategies

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm glad you talked about the land bank.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That was my bill.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How long do you think it's going to take to put the committee together?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Obviously, it feeds off of the individuals who are not paying their taxes, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: In other words, it has to do with liens and figuring out how to deal with positives so people don't lose their homes, et cetera, sort of alternatives to...

utterances.text: People losing their homes.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So my question is, I think you used the word amnesty or maybe you said we're not going to do amnesty.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I wanted to understand if that is possible.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And then just going forward.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Even though hopefully you'll collect the 573, what are the ideas?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you continuing to use collection agencies?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Do they come through with what they promise?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How are we going to keep, I mean, there's a guy in my area, he puts up something, I swear, on every single lamppost, right?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And it's really hard to get off.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You can't get it off.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: He shouldn't be doing that, and he should be fined, and he should pay you fines.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That kind of thing.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So we did do the 30-day review internally just to see if we could improve any processes so that we can better collect.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: One of those things that we have identified is that most of the majority of ECB collections are most responsive when we're able to reach out to them directly within the first year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So one of the priorities for us is for our account executive unit to do that.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The first year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, try to increase the first year outreach as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: We do still utilize the collection agencies, and that's primarily through mail and phone calls, but it's important that for us internally we start and try to target the first year.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: With the uncollectible population, we are right now trying to re-review it.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: There are a couple of data.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: strategies that we're trying to implement.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: The 744 that you talked about.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, just to see if there are any additional information coming in that would then allow us to restart enforcement on them.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: So when you did the great work with Amazon, the 10 million, was that something that had been sitting around for a while or was that something that was like the first year type of situation?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that has been a violation that's been sitting around for a while and that was the largest ECB, single ECB violation source.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: sort of like how you do with any other constituent, actually.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We did one-on-one review of their idling violations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We connected it to any questions they might have, any challenges they might have, and worked them through the entire process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that was really an example of how we can work sort of in tandem.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Do you think some of the other carriers, there are quite a few of them, have similar violations that could be handled in the same way?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I know the project, I guess it's under parking violations, but the issue is, you know, I'm making this up, you get, you know, 100 violations, you can put them in a pot and you only pay a percentage.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I don't think that's terrible, some people do, but the point is, are they paying it?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It's one thing to get a discount, it's another thing not to even pay the discount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I do have the list of the highest, the next highest violations by category.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I don't have them with me right now, but I'm happy to sort of reconnect.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: I'd love to hear that because I do think they need to pay.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Some of them are getting discounts.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: It's not like they're paying their full share.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so right now the strategies that we utilize for Amazon, we're just now going down the list, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're going down the list, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Number two, three, four, five, six, just focusing on them right now to do that one-on-one personal sort of work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I would like to see that list.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're happy to talk to you more about that and the strategies that we're utilizing.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.text: And we provided letters, and we also worked closely with OLR to address it with their impacted labor unions.

chapters.summary: Linda Lee opens the final portion of the day's hearings, welcoming IBO Director Louisa Chafee and her team. Committee counsel swears in Chafee and officers Parker and Subramanian.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens IBO hearing portion and swears in Director Chafee

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, hello, oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so good afternoon and welcome to the final portion of today's last day of our executive budget hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee and welcome to our director.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, what?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I don't know what happened.

utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.text: So welcome to Director Louisa Chaffee and your team, and thank you all for joining us today to answer questions.

utterances.text: I do.

chapters.summary: Chafee presents IBO's tax revenue forecast aligning with administration projections, noting a $1.1 billion gap in the $124.7 billion budget that can be addressed during adoption. She highlights the two-year extension of the class size law deadline and teacher bonus costs of $21 million.
chapters.title: Director Chafee on IBO's FY27 budget overview and $1.1 billion gap

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Okay, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Council Finance Committee Chair Lee and members of the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: I'm Louisa Chafee, Director of the Independent Budget Office, and with me today are Sarah Parker and Sarita Subramanian, IBO's Senior Research and Strategy Officers.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And do you all hear the echo?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Okay, it's just me.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think this room is just, doesn't have the best acoustics.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: No problem, I just, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So thank you so much for the opportunity to testify on Mayor Zoram Kwame Wandame's

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: This testimony is a condensed version of our submitted testimony.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: The late state budget presented challenges to New York City, but the funding included in the state budget aligns quite closely with the administration's projections for the current fiscal year and for 2027.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: For 2027, IBO's tax revenue forecast also aligns closely with the administration's projections.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Within the total $124.7 billion budget, IBO projects a gap of $1.1 billion, a gap that can easily be addressed during the budget adoption process.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: On the last day of session, a two-year extension of the deadline to implement the state class size law was passed.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Including, or thereafter, it became clear that there would be bonuses for up to 2,450 teachers whose classes exceeded the class size caps.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Which IBO estimates will require an additional $21 million.

chapters.summary: Chafee reports 110,000 children receive child care vouchers with 25,000 on a waitlist since the portal closed in June 2025. She estimates the city needs $78 million more in 2027, rising to nearly $1 billion in 2028, and notes the state set up a separate housing voucher program yielding $33 million for about 900-1,100 vouchers.
chapters.title: Director Chafee on child care voucher shortfall and CityFHEPS funding

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Meanwhile, IBO notes that the administration's assumptions that it can achieve $149 million in annual savings in the area of due process cases also remains untested.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: New York City provides means-tested child care vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Generally, services are accessed through a portal.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Currently, close to 110,000 children

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: receive such vouchers, and there are over 25,000 children on the wait list because the portal has been closed since June of 2025.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: The executive budget contains an unexpected fiscal shortfall as funding drops from $1.8 billion in 2026 to $818 million annually starting in 2028.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: IBO estimates the city needs an additional $78 million in 2027, rising to almost $1 billion annually in 2028 to address this shortfall.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And if the portal were to be reopened or the waitlist addressed, even more funds would be needed.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: The state...

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Did not provide any funds for the City FEPS program, but instead set up its own separate housing voucher access program, which will yield about $33 million

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: in funding for the city and support approximately 900 to 1,100 vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: This is a year-to-year program.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: IBO does not expect the administration to reduce CityFAPS funding as a result of this new state program.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And as a reminder, CityFAPS is budgeted at $2 billion, and the executive budget reflects a plan to achieve $235 million annually in unspecified administrative streamlining.

chapters.summary: Chafee says Tier 6 enhancements require $41 million more in 2027 growing to $69 million by 2030, and notes IBO cannot evaluate pied-a-terre revenue due to unknown variables. She highlights federal risks including 43,500 New Yorkers losing SNAP benefits and 450,000 statewide losing health insurance from Essential Plan changes.
chapters.title: Director Chafee on pension costs, pied-a-terre uncertainty, and federal funding risks

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Next on to city pensions.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: IBO projects, based on estimates from the city chief actuary,

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: The state's new Tier 6 enhancements will require additional funding above that that is in the executive budget, $41 million in 2027, growing to $69 million in 2030.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Based on the actuary's analysis of a prior proposal, IBO finds that the administration's proposed extending pension liability payments to be consistent with sound practices.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: IBO cannot evaluate the executive budget's assumption around the so-called pied-a-terre tax, as the amount of revenue will depend on variables such as how are property values calculated,

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: how will the city administer collections, and how will second property owners behave in relationship to the surcharge.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So finally, it is difficult to overstate the pressures and uncertainties weighing on the city during this budget process based on the federal administration's global actions and policies, as well as dramatic cutbacks in key programs.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: For example, about 43,500 New Yorkers are at high risk of losing their Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, their SNAP benefits, in the near term.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And the relatively modest increases included in the state and executive budget of millions are likely insufficient to address the rising food insecurity.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: As many as 450,000 people statewide, half of them living in New Yorkers,

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: will lose health insurance due to federal changes undercutting the state's essential plan.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Neither the state budget nor the executive budget addressed this health care access loss.

chapters.summary: Chafee reports the executive budget increases building energy funding by $376 million in 2027 rising to $477 million by 2030, bringing total energy costs to $1.3 billion. She notes the administration continues improving budget accuracy and IBO looks forward to analyzing chief savings officer proposals and the new Commission on Government Efficiency.
chapters.title: Director Chafee on energy cost increases and budget accuracy under Mamdani administration

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Meanwhile, with the ongoing war in Iran, the resulting economic uncertainties, and sharply rising energy costs, the executive budget increases funding for power and energy by $376 million in 2027,

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: rising to $477 million in 2030.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: This brings the total citywide budget for building energy costs up to $1.3 billion.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So finally, IBO again notes that the Mondami administration continues its practice of more accurately reflecting the city's expenses.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Given the challenges ahead, IBO also looks forward to being able to thoroughly analyze the proposals from the chief savings officers, as well as the work of the Commission on Government Efficiency, COGE, the new Charter Revision Commission.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and we're happy to take your questions.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether IBO agrees with the administration's $122 million DOE plan for 70% class size compliance. Sarah Parker confirms alignment, and notes IBO has not updated its full cost estimate for 100% compliance by 2030 but plans to do so with new data.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on class size compliance costs and DOE funding alignment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by Councilmember Mercedes Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for sticking through to the end.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so in terms of class size, I know that you had outlined in your testimony, you had mentioned some of the incentives that UFT announced and how

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know, it still is.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: a bit difficult to sort of assess the true cost of this, but I know the administration planned 122 million in DOE to meet the 70% compliance next school year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So do you think that, are you in agreement with that number or?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with the newly extended timeline, does IBO have a new cost estimate to meet 100% class size compliance by 2030?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: No, we haven't updated our estimate.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: We plan to do that as we have when we get new data.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: But based on our current estimate and the funding in the budget, we do feel that that is in alignment with what we would anticipate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then the executive plan does include the funding for class size bonus payments, which you had mentioned, which is good to see.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But how many, how many, wait, actually, let me move on to the next question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about IBO's Fair Fares expansion report. Sarah Parker explains five scenarios including expanded discounts and expanded eligibility to 200-400% of federal poverty line, using ridership trends and census data, noting that higher-income eligible individuals commute at higher rates than current participants.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on IBO Fair Fares expansion cost estimates and assumptions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Moving on to fair fares.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On June 4th, 2026, IBO released a report reviewing and estimating costs on multiple fare fares expansion scenarios.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we would like to learn more about how IBO reached these estimates, the assumptions used, and the feasibility of some of these scenarios.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you could walk us through your analysis and the factors and assumptions that you had considered.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So before I turn it to my colleague, Sarah, I'd like to thank you for your attention to our Fair Fares report and also point out

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: That on the same day, we published a report on free buses to address all facets of potential transportation affordability.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: That said, we are briefing the council, and we also have a webinar coming up on June 16th.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Oh, great.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Sarah?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: I'm going to take that one.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So, yeah, so we did, as you mentioned, estimate five different scenarios, and two of those expanded the discount that's offered to eligible

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: participants, and then three scenarios expand the eligibility criteria to...

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: The federal poverty line, so 200%, 300%, and 400% of the federal poverty line.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: And so in doing this analysis, we used a variety of data based on current ridership trends and usage trends,

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: as well as census data to estimate, particularly for the scenarios that would increase eligibility, what would be the expected increased use of transit, because those individuals

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: tend to commute at higher rates than those that are currently eligible for the program.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: And so as Louisa mentioned, we have a lot of details in our report.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: We have a briefing that we're offering.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: People can sign up on our website.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: But happy to answer any more specific questions you might have.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the wide enrollment and utilization ranges in IBO's estimates and whether marketing funding is needed. Parker notes current usage is relatively low because eligible individuals are less likely to commute, and that Fair Fares already has one of the larger marketing budgets among HRA programs, with enrollment barriers in the Access NYC system.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Fair Fares enrollment ranges and marketing awareness

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think, so the complexity of estimating the cost of expanding fare fares is, like you, as you mentioned, it's tricky because it's related to and linked to client enrollment levels and the transit utilization,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: which are definitely factors that can make

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: the amounts fluctuate quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I know you had estimated a range of 19% to 32% for enrollment levels and then of eligible people and 19% to 63% for their transit utilization.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you just mentioned that they tend to utilize transportation more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so how did you come to that finding as well as the wide range?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And which do you feel are most likely?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think we wanted to provide a broad range because there are a lot of different options that are on the table and ideas that people have for expansion.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So IBO doesn't take a position on any one of them, but we do note that we

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: very clearly lay out our assumptions for, for example, take-up rates at each of those levels, as well as, like I mentioned, assuming an increased usage.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: I do want to note that in our report we also note that current usage is relatively low, and largely because those that are eligible are less likely to commute.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: And so we take a variety of factors into account.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Account as well as I think importantly looking at those that are eligible for other MTA fair discount programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that this is something that on the council is very important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And one of the things that came up several times throughout the budget hearings was the fact that it seems like a lot of folks still don't know about this program or if they're eligible or where they can find out more or sign up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So one of the things I think that was proposed is putting some marketing money towards this to make sure that people are aware.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so wanted to know if IBO had done any sort of research in terms of like, should there be additional funding put towards making sure that folks know about this program and marketing dollars, what would be appropriate amount?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: That's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: We do note in our report, actually, we looked at marketing costs relative to other HRA programs, and actually Fair Fairs has one of the larger budgets in terms of marketing.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: We do also note, and you mentioned determining eligibility and some of the challenges in enrolling, and that's something that we also note in our report.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: For those that are not, it's a little bit easier for those that are in access NYC already, but there are a lot of reporting requirements for those that enter through the open enrollment process.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: And so we note that those might be barriers to enrolling in the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about natural partnerships to expand Fair Fares awareness. Parker suggests Department of Finance income data sharing as a potential option. Chafee raises concerns about Access NYC barriers and suggests automatic notification through tax filing, similar to how DOF handled earned income tax credit outreach.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Fair Fares partnerships and interagency data sharing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Actually, just based off of what you just said, what would be some natural partnerships that may be able to expand eligibility or help folks know about it?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because we have a lot of municipal workers in the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have a lot of, even just within our own city employees, I wonder, as well as if they can help spread the word, if there's increased eligibility there, or if there is...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know, a way to use nonprofit organizations and partnerships to be able to spread the word on that.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Well, in terms of, you know, sort of interagency data sharing, one thing that we thought might be helpful to look at is Department of Finance has access to income.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: I know that's very challenging in terms of data from a data sharing perspective, but that is something that we point to as a potential option that could reduce some of the barriers that people might face.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: We are particularly concerned about the barriers of navigating Access NYC.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Considering the changes with the reductions in SNAP eligibility and the lack of access to health care, there may be even fewer people using Access NYC and thus more people eligible for Fair Fares but not using the system.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And as Fair Fares is a city tax levy program and thus it is at the city's discretion how to administer it, there might be other models in the city.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: to how to distribute such a benefit.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And the example that came to mind, as Sarita illuminated, was when the Department of Finance did the earned income tax credit and

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: appropriately found the information via state law to inform people that they were eligible for an earned income tax credit.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: People in filing their tax returns demonstrate their eligibility for fair fares.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: It could be that people would be automatically notified.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, that's a good idea.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how the administration should assess budgetary needs for Fair Fares expansion given forecasting challenges. Chafee references a request for 35 additional headcount and $800,000 for Access NYC, and suggests using benefits calculators and technology to reduce paperwork assessment burdens.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on budgeting for Fair Fares expansion overhead and Access NYC navigation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then at the General Welfare Executive Budget Hearing, DSS Commissioner Dalton explained that estimating expansion costs was very complicated and that additional overhead funding may be needed depending on enrollment and utilization rates.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So given how challenging it is to forecast the impact of various program expansions, how does IBO advise that the administration should assess the budgetary needs for expansion?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: You mean how much money should be put into Access NYC to assist in having Fair Fares participants navigate the system?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: I believe there was a request for 35 additional headcount to work Access NYC and $800,000 for...

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: I'm looking at Sarita because she's meant to say yes or no.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Memory serves, those may be the correct numbers.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: I think that we would be really happy to talk with you at longer length about

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: how a benefits calculator can be used to utilize technology and assist people and have the important humans do more outreach and less assessment of paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good answer.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether IBO identified key capital investment areas for NYCHA beyond the $90 million for vacant unit readiness. Sarita Subramanian explains NYCHA's separate federal capital funding and details the city's capital subsidies for public housing including asbestos abatement, elevator, boiler, and roof repairs.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on NYCHA capital needs and block-by-block housing plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then just really quickly, this is my last question around the block by block, which is the mayor's new housing plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It outlines several key objectives and tools for addressing the citywide housing shortage.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The report discusses several key agencies, including HPD, DOB, NYCHA, and DCP.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So for NYCHA, for example, while the executive capital commitment plan includes $90 million of additional capital funding for Section 9 vacant unit readiness, this amount is dwarfed by the additional $500 million for packed conversions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has IBO identified other key capital investment areas that could drive down NYCHA's total?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Capital need.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: So NYCHA receives capital funding from the federal government directly, and that's on an annual basis, and that is budgeted separately within NYCHA's capital budget.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: So that's separate from what's in the city's capital budget.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: But within the city's capital budget, there is funding for the vacant readiness.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: It's currently budgeted through fiscal year 2028.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: And this is part of a larger bucket of city capital subsidies for public housing, which has about $1.2 billion in fiscal year 2026,

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: $836 million in 2027, and $210 million in 2028.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: So these funds are for capital work such as asbestos and lead abatement.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: elevator, boiler, and roof repairs.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: One thing that we always note is that one of the things with NIH's capital budget is that it is subject to both the ability to hire up contractors to get construction projects started.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: And so with any capital budget, there's a certain amount of money that

utterances.speaker: Sarita Subramanian
utterances.text: that is budgeted in one year that might be rolled into the next year, and that is quite typical.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm just going to pass off to Councilmember Narcisse that has a couple questions.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks why the homeless services budget exceeds the migrant crisis peak despite falling arrivals, and whether H&H's financial plan can withstand federal cuts. Parker says daily shelter rates remain elevated, and Chafee notes concern about 450,000 people losing Essential Plan coverage and seeking care at H&H with no budgeted funds to address it.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on homeless services budget and H&H federal cuts

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Quickly, because we've got to run.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The homeless services budget now tops the migrant crisis peak, even as arrivals fell.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What does IBO's analysis say?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Can you explain that?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Public hospitals face federal cuts, right?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And there are hundreds of millions while the city closed back their pension saving.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does IBO see H&H financial plan holding up?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Sarita, why don't you speak to the asylum part?

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So, I'm sorry, your question was about the Department of Homeless Services budget still remaining high despite the asylum seekers.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So that is something we've been tracking really closely and particularly tracking the daily rate that they are paying for shelter remains relatively high.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: It was even more elevated during the asylum seeker crisis.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: There were expectations that that would decline, and I think even in IBO's projections, we had factored that in, and we have not seen the level of decline for that daily rate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we're also seeing a large population of single adults and adult families that has really contributed to the elevated costs.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And I believe the second part of your question had to do with the changes in the federal funding and the impact on health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So the Independent Budget Office does not directly receive health and hospitals financials, but we are aware of the city's contributions.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And as I outlined in our testimony, we are very concerned about the

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: soon-to-be-occurring circumstance of up to 450,000 people no longer being eligible for the state's essential plan

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: and continuing to need legitimate health care and seeking services, and that this may have a very large impact on H&H.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And as we flagged, neither the state nor the city added any funding in their budgets for this circumstance.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks which agencies IBO believes are underfunded. Chafee identifies Department of Corrections as not having enough funding, and notes IBO's limited access to H&H financials but coordination to obtain detailed statements, with options like alternate insurance or expanded free clinics to fill coverage gaps.
chapters.title: Council Member Narcisse on underfunded agencies and Department of Corrections

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many agencies that you realize that's under budget, if you have to say, right now, from the budget plan?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You have any agencies specifically that you can say that under budget?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Where we believe that the agency receives additional funding or should receive it.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: We're concerned about Department of Corrections not having enough funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, that's...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I would also add to what Louisa said.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have federal changes, local changes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: report specifically focusing on H&H.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we do get access, or we coordinate with them to get their detailed financial statements, so we have that information.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we are pretty limited in the amount of information we have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But also in that report, we talk about, there are a couple of different ways that the city, the state could fill that gap.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They could provide alternate insurance for individuals, or they could expand the free clinic options that are available.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we fully recognize that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Those are maybe at capacity and would require substantial additional funds, but that is an option that we point out in the report.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: Time is up for us.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether the mayor's block-by-block housing plan sufficiently addresses distressed housing. Parker describes IBO's analysis of financial and physical distress indicators, noting rent-stabilized buildings fare equal to or better than non-stabilized rentals, and distressed properties require different policy tools depending on their specific challenges.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on IBO rent-stabilized housing distress report and mayor's housing plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just really quickly, going back to block by block, I know that you recently published a report, Current State of Rent-Stabilized Housing, which provides an overview of the distressed housing stock in New York.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in your opinion, does the mayor's housing plan sufficiently address the rehabilitation and preservation of distressed housing in the city?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If not, what additional strategies should the city implement?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: So we did publish two reports, and we also testified to the Rent Guidelines Board.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Chafee
utterances.text: And I'm going to ask Sarah to speak to the details.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So specifically, IBO looked at multiple indicators of distress for buildings with rent-stabilized apartments in them.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: We looked at both signs of financial distress, looking at participation and eligibility for the lien sale, and also physical distress.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So housing code violations and participation in the HPD alternative enforcement program and emergency repair program.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: Distressed properties are not a monolith, and different properties require different policy tools and interventions, whether they're facing financial distress or physical distress.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: But one key takeaway from our analysis is that among rent-stabilized apartments, rent-stabilized buildings fare equal to or, in some cases, better than non-stabilized rental buildings across these multiple metrics of distress.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: So it's not something specific to rent stabilization.

utterances.speaker: Sarah Parker
utterances.text: It is a small subset of housing, more specifically citywide.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Linda Lee wraps up the IBO portion and formally concludes all agency budget hearings for the FY2027 executive budget, thanking IBO for their thorough analysis and announcing follow-up on remaining questions.
chapters.title: Chair Lee concludes executive budget hearings for FY27

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for making that distinction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and we have several other questions, but I think we're going to have to follow up with you guys and look forward to the answers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you guys are always super, super thorough and helpful in your analysis.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much, and thanks for being here with us until the very end.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I am going to conclude the formal agency budget hearings for Executive 27 budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yay!

summary: NYC Council Finance Committee holds executive budget hearings for FY2027, questioning Department of Finance Commissioner Richard Lee on property tax reform, ECB debt collections, savings, and agency operations.
Council members question DOF on racial equity, deed theft prevention, lien sale pause, pied-a-terre surcharge, and Section 1127 miscalculation affecting 40,000 city employees.
Independent Budget Office Director Louisa Chafee testifies on FY2027 budget gaps, class size costs, Fair Fares expansion, child care vouchers, NYCHA capital needs, and federal funding risks.
title: Committee on Finance
